text: Asking why we study the cosmos, and for whom, keeps the field honest and keeps the sky something that belongs to everyone, not just to whoever owns the biggest telescope.
Because if an Earth microbe hitches a ride and we later find life on Mars, we may never be sure it wasn't ours.
What if our universe is not "real" in the usual sense, but an incredibly complex simulation run by an advanced intelligence?
That's not detoxing—that's dehydration with branding.
The dose makes the poison—even water, sugar, or oxygen can kill you at the wrong dose.
You don't need a PhD to spot nonsense. Just a bit of logic, a little healthy skepticism, and the courage to say "uh, that doesn't sound right."
A definition that keeps updating to exclude every counterexample has stopped being a definition.
You infer that I understand things from my behavior, and that's the only evidence anyone has ever had about another mind.
We built machines that talk before we agreed on what talking means.
A galaxy could host millions of civilizations serially and still be silent at any given moment.
Loneliness might be a scheduling problem as much as a rarity problem.
The silence is data. The sensible response is to treat it like any other measurement, and to build a much bigger telescope.
Entanglement went from metaphysical embarrassment to engineering resource. The entire field I work in exists because some people refused to shut up.
At that point you've left physics for something more like theology with Hilbert spaces.
The universe doesn't owe us interpretability, but demanding it anyway has paid off every time so far.
I work in quantum technology. Which is exactly why I'm asking you to be more skeptical of it.
"Within five years" has been true for twenty years.
A field that can say "we don't know yet" out loud is easier to trust.
